atopicDermatitis.presentation: Intensely pruritic, eczematous plaques; chronic-relapsing; FLEXURAL distribution (antecubital/popliteal fossae, neck, wrists) in older children/adults; cheeks and extensor surfaces in infants; lichenification from chronic scratching; associated with atopic triad (asthma, allergic rhinitis)
atopicDermatitis.itchScratchCycle: Pruritus → scratching → skin barrier disruption → secondary colonization (S. aureus in >90%) → worsening inflammation → worsening pruritus
atopicDermatitis.diagnosis: Clinical diagnosis; no specific lab test. Elevated IgE and eosinophilia supportive but non-specific.
atopicDermatitis.severity: Mild: limited areas, infrequent itching; Moderate: widespread, frequent itch, sleep disruption; Severe: widespread, constant itch, significant QoL impact

foundation_allStages: Emollients: cornerstone of all AD management; apply within 3 minutes of bathing (soak-and-seal); thick creams or ointments (CeraVe, Vanicream, petroleum jelly) preferred over lotions
Trigger avoidance: identify and avoid soaps/detergents, specific foods (if confirmed), environmental allergens, wool
Gentle skin care: lukewarm water, fragrance-free detergents, pat dry
mild_moderate_topical: Topical corticosteroids: dose frequency and potency based on severity and location; hydrocortisone 1% for face/flexures; triamcinolone 0.1% for trunk/extremities; fluocinonide or clobetasol for thick or resistant plaques — use pulse dosing to maintain remission

Topical calcineurin inhibitors (TCIs): tacrolimus 0.03-0.1% ointment, pimecrolimus 1% cream — steroid-sparing; safe for face and eyelids; first-line for sensitive areas; black box warning for theoretical malignancy risk (clinical significance uncertain)
Crisaborole (Eucrisa): PDE-4 inhibitor; mild-moderate AD; twice daily; low side-effect profile
moderate_severe_systemic: Dupilumab (Dupixent): IL-4/IL-13 inhibitor; first biologic approved for AD; 300 mg SC every 2 weeks; dramatically effective; ophthalmology follow-up (conjunctivitis side effect); approved ≥6 months old
JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib — Rinvoq; abrocitinib — Cibinqo): oral; very effective; monitor CBC, lipids, liver; not first-line for most; risk of serious infections, malignancy
Cyclosporine: off-label; rapid onset; bridge to biologic
Phototherapy: narrowband UVB; effective for widespread moderate AD
biologic_referralCriteria: Inadequate response to topical therapy over 3-6 months
Moderate-severe disease with significant QoL impact (SCORAD >50 or POEM >16)
Frequent flares requiring systemic steroids (>2 courses/year)
Refer to dermatology or allergy/immunology for dupilumab initiation and monitoring

atopicDermatitis.infection_management: S. aureus colonization is nearly universal — decolonization bleach baths (0.5 cup per full bathtub, 2-3x/week) reduce flares; treat secondary impetiginization with mupirocin topically (localized) or dicloxacillin/cephalexin orally (widespread)

managementApproach.NP_role: Initiate and escalate topical therapy; counsel on skin care fundamentals; treat acute flares; identify complications (secondary infection, contact dermatitis, erythroderma); refer to dermatology for moderate-severe disease, diagnostic uncertainty, or biologic need
managementApproach.commonError: Under-treating with hydrocortisone 0.5-1% for all patients regardless of severity — insufficient potency leads to chronic under-control and eventual extensive disease

redFlagsEscalation: Erythroderma: >90% BSA involvement → ED (thermoregulation failure, sepsis risk)
Eczema herpeticum (Kaposi varicelliform eruption): punched-out, clustered vesicular lesions + fever in AD patient → suspect HSV superinfection → acyclovir or valacyclovir; may require hospitalization
Generalized pustular psoriasis: acute eruption of sterile pustules + fever + systemic illness → dermatology emergency
Psoriatic arthritis joint destruction: aggressive joint swelling + elevated markers → rheumatology urgently

populationConsiderations.adult: Assess work and QoL impact — AD and psoriasis affect employment (visible lesions), relationships, and sleep; address psychological burden; depression screening with PHQ-9 at diagnosis and annually
populationConsiderations.chronic_disease: Both conditions are lifelong; patient education on maintenance therapy (not just acute treatment) is essential to prevent the boom-bust cycle of intensive treatment → stop → relapse

followUpAndMonitoring.psoriasis: 4-8 week follow-up after initiating topical therapy; MTX: CBC + LFTs every 4-12 weeks; annual metabolic/cardiovascular screening; skin cancer screening
followUpAndMonitoring.AD: 2-4 week follow-up during flares; 3-6 monthly when stable; review trigger diary; monitor for secondary infection; ophthalmology annually if on dupilumab

lessons.topicName: Smoking Cessation: Pharmacotherapy Options and Counseling Strategies

lesson.clinicalFraming: Tobacco use is the leading preventable cause of death in the US, responsible for 480,000 deaths/year. Smoking cessation is the single most cost-effective intervention across virtually every chronic disease. NPs have both the opportunity and the clinical mandate to address tobacco at every encounter. Brief cessation counseling in primary care combined with pharmacotherapy triples quit rates compared to willpower alone.

5A_framework.Ask: Ask about tobacco use at EVERY visit — smoking status should be documented as a vital sign
5A_framework.Advise: Provide clear, personalized advice to quit: 'Quitting is the most important thing you can do for your health right now'
5A_framework.Assess: Assess readiness to quit: 'Are you thinking about quitting in the next 30 days?' — motivational interviewing for those not ready
5A_framework.Assist: Provide pharmacotherapy + behavioral support; set a quit date; brief counseling or refer to Quitline (1-800-QUIT-NOW)
5A_framework.Arrange: Follow-up contact within 1 week of quit date; 1 month; 3 months; 6 months

FRAMES: Feedback: personalized risk (spirometry, cardiac risk, etc.)
Menu: offer a choice of cessation methods
Self-efficacy: reinforce patient's ability to succeed
motivationalInterviewing.forPrecontemplation: Avoid confrontation; explore pros and cons of smoking; plant a seed; document 5R discussion (Relevance, Risks, Rewards, Roadblocks, Repetition)

vareniclien_Chantix.mechanism: Partial alpha-4-beta-2 nicotinic receptor agonist — reduces withdrawal symptoms AND reduces reward from smoking
vareniclien_Chantix.efficacy: Most effective single agent — 2-3x quit rate vs placebo; superior to NRT monotherapy and bupropion
vareniclien_Chantix.dosing: 0.5 mg daily days 1-3 → 0.5 mg BID days 4-7 → 1 mg BID thereafter; start 1 week before target quit date; 12-week standard course; extend to 24 weeks for additional benefit
vareniclien_Chantix.safety: EAGLES trial definitively cleared cardiovascular safety concerns; neuropsychiatric safety acceptable; monitor for nausea (most common side effect — take with food and water); vivid dreams; mood monitoring recommended
vareniclien_Chantix.contraindications: End-stage renal disease (significant dose reduction: 0.5 mg daily); use with caution in patients with psychiatric illness — monitor mood, behavior changes
vareniclien_Chantix.renal_dosing: CrCl 30-60: start at 0.5 mg daily → max 0.5 mg BID; CrCl <30: max 0.5 mg daily

bupropion_Wellbutrin_Zyban.mechanism: Dopamine and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor; reduces nicotine withdrawal and craving
bupropion_Wellbutrin_Zyban.efficacy: ~1.5-2x quit rate vs placebo; less effective than varenicline
bupropion_Wellbutrin_Zyban.dosing: 150 mg daily days 1-3 → 150 mg BID (at least 8 hours apart) starting day 4; begin 1-2 weeks before quit date; 7-12 weeks standard
bupropion_Wellbutrin_Zyban.advantage: Can be used in patients with comorbid depression; prevents post-cessation weight gain (modest effect)
bupropion_Wellbutrin_Zyban.contraindications: Seizure disorder, eating disorder (anorexia/bulimia — lowers seizure threshold), MAOI use within 14 days, abrupt alcohol/benzo discontinuation
bupropion_Wellbutrin_Zyban.sideEffects: Insomnia (give second dose by 2 PM), dry mouth, agitation; do NOT take at bedtime

NRT_nicotineReplacementTherapy.mechanism: Delivers nicotine without tobacco toxins; reduces withdrawal symptoms

forms.patch: 21 mg, 14 mg, 7 mg; wear 16-24 hours; change site daily; effective for background craving; takes 2-4 hours to peak
forms.gum: 2 mg (<25 cigarettes/day) or 4 mg (≥25 cigarettes/day); chew-and-park technique; 9-15 pieces/day max; avoid eating/drinking 15 min before use
forms.lozenge: Similar dosing to gum; do not chew; dissolves in 20-30 min
forms.inhaler: Prescription; mimics hand-to-mouth behavior; delivers 4 mg/cartridge; 6-16 cartridges/day
forms.nasal_spray: Fastest onset (10 min); 0.5 mg/dose; 1-2 doses/hour; preferred for high-dependency smokers

NRT_nicotineReplacementTherapy.combination_NRT: Patch (long-acting) + gum or lozenge (short-acting) = most effective NRT strategy; ~60% more effective than patch alone
NRT_nicotineReplacementTherapy.OTC_availability: Patch, gum, lozenge available OTC; inhaler and nasal spray require prescription

pharmacotherapy.combinationTherapy: Varenicline + NRT patch or bupropion + NRT — some evidence for improved quit rates; acceptable and reasonable in motivated patients with multiple prior quit attempts

managementApproach.quitDateSetting: Set a specific quit date within 1-2 weeks; remove cigarettes and smoking paraphernalia from home and car on quit date; anticipate high-risk situations
managementApproach.quitline: 1-800-QUIT-NOW: free, evidence-based telephone counseling + free NRT in most states; refer all patients
managementApproach.electronicCigarettes: Not FDA-approved cessation tools; evidence insufficient to recommend; use as harm reduction only if patient refuses other methods and unable to quit; counsel on lack of long-term safety data
managementApproach.relapsePrevention: Most ex-smokers relapse multiple times before sustained abstinence; normalize this; celebrate any period of abstinence; reassess pharmacotherapy; do NOT express disappointment

redFlagsEscalation: Varenicline: new onset or worsening depression, suicidal ideation, aggressive behavior → stop medication; refer to mental health
Bupropion: new seizure → stop immediately; ER evaluation
NRT in active ACS: use with caution — stop if chest pain worsens; NRT is safer than continued smoking but requires monitoring

populationConsiderations.adult: Individualize pharmacotherapy choice based on comorbidities: cardiac disease → NRT patch + lozenge; psychiatric comorbidity → bupropion or varenicline with close monitoring; renal failure → dose-adjust varenicline; pregnancy → NRT preferred over pharmacotherapy but smoking is more harmful
populationConsiderations.preventive_care: Update cessation attempt history at each visit; avoid stigmatizing language ('smoker' vs 'person who smokes'); link cessation to patient's specific chronic disease burden (COPD, CVD, T2DM)

followUpAndMonitoring.week1: Contact within 1 week of quit date — highest relapse period; assess withdrawal symptoms, medication tolerability, adherence
followUpAndMonitoring.month1: Assess abstinence; if relapsed: discuss barriers, adjust pharmacotherapy; if abstinent: reinforce, address triggers, plan for extended therapy if needed
followUpAndMonitoring.month3and6: Sustained abstinence confirmation; weight monitoring (average 5-10 lb gain with cessation — address with PA and dietary guidance); mental health monitoring
followUpAndMonitoring.extendedTherapy: Varenicline 24-week course for those at high risk of relapse; combination NRT indefinitely preferred over return to smoking
